segments.s285-g185: برفتند is the past 3rd person plural 'they departed/went' (verb before its complements, a common poetic order). با کشتگان is a prepositional phrase 'with the slain' (با = 'with', کشتگان = plural participle 'the killed, the slain') attached to that action. همچنان is an adverb 'in the same way/just so' that modifies the whole clause, linking this action to what came before.
segments.s285-g186: گروی here is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'a troop/group' (equivalent to modern گروهی) and functions as a noun identifying who is affected. زره را is 'the armor/armored (men)' with را marking it as the definite direct object; زره is used metonymically for armored fighters. پیاده is an adverbial/manner phrase 'on foot' placed before the verb, and دوان is a past action 'made/run/put to flight' (they drove/made [them] run). The subject is omitted and understood as the same 'they' from برفتند, so the clause means 'they drove the armored troop to run on foot.'
segments.s285-g41: به کردار نخچیر در پیش من is a prepositional phrase describing manner and location: به + noun phrase (کردار نخچیر) = 'in the manner/behavior of hunted game'; در پیش من = 'in my presence/before me'. The word order is poetic (manner phrase fronted) and کردار here means 'demeanor/way of acting'.
segments.s285-g42: کجات is the interrogative 'where (are/has)...?'. آن سپاه‌ای is 'that host/army' (poetic pluralizing/construct), and سر انجمن 'the head(s) of the assembly' — سر used in the sense of 'chief/leader'. The clause asks, rhetorically, for the location/status of that army and its leaders.
segments.s285-g43: نیامد is the simple past negative 'did not come'. ز لشکر = 'from the host/army' (poetic preposition z = az). ترا is the object 'to you' split here into the 2nd-person clitic ت plus the object marker را. یار کس = 'any friend/companion' (یار = companion; کس = person/anyone). The word order is subject-verb omitted (implied 'no one') + oblique phrase.
segments.s285-g44: وز here is the poetic juxtaposition of و + ز (and from), introducing the next clause 'and from them...'. ایشان = 'them/they'. نبینمت is present-subjunctive/negative fused form meaning literally 'I do not see you' (ne + binam + -t clitic), but used here as 'I see no rescuer among them'. فریادرس is a substantival compound 'rescuer/succor'.
segments.s285-g45: کجات is again the interrogative 'where?'. آنهمه is a quantifier 'all that/so much'. زور و مردانگی list two abstract qualities: زور = 'strength/might' and مردانگی = 'bravery/valor'. The phrase rhetorically questions the absence of these qualities.
segments.s285-g46: This segment continues the catalogue with nouns joined by و (and): سلیح 'weapons', دل 'heart/spirit', گنج 'treasure/wealth', فرزانگی 'wisdom/sagacity'. The simple coordination emphasizes the list of missing resources/virtues.
segments.s285-g47: ستون گوان is a compound-like expression: ستون 'pillar/support' + گوان 'like/as' = 'pillar-like' or 'pillars'. پشت افراسیاب literally 'the back of Afrasiab' here means 'Afrasiab's support/behind' (i.e., his supporters). The phrase names the once-solid supporters of Afrasiab.
segments.s285-g48: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. شاه را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker attached to 'the king'. تیره گشت is the compound of adjective + past 'became dark/ominous' (تیره = dark/ominous, گشت = 'became'). آفتاب 'the sun' is the subject of becoming dark—a poetic way to say the king's fortunes have clouded.
segments.s285-g121: همی here is a frequentative/adverb meaning 'continually' or 'kept on'. زار is an adverbial/epithet meaning 'wretchedly' or 'in mourning', and بگریست is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb ('he/it wept' or 'wailed'); together زار بگریست forms the verbal phrase 'wailed (in grief)'. لشکر همه follows after the verb in typical poetic word order and names the agent/subject ('the entire army/host').
segments.s285-g122: ز + verbal noun/infinitive (نادیدن) gives the causal sense 'because of (not seeing)'. نادیدن here functions as a noun/gerund meaning 'not seeing' or 'failure to see'. پهلوان is the object of that gerund ('the hero'), and رمه ('throng') is the actor that failed to see him; the whole phrase means 'because the throng had not seen the hero.'
segments.s285-g123: درفشی پدید آمد is a simple past clause: درفشی (the banner/standard) + پدید آمد ('appeared, came into view'). از introduces the origin 'from/out of', and تیره گرد is a compound poetic phrase meaning a dark/black cloud or gathering (تیره = dark; گرد in the image = dust/cloud), so 'a banner appeared from a dark cloud/cluster.'
segments.s285-g124: This segment names what composes that dark cluster: گرازان و تازان links two plural noun-forms ('boars' and 'raiders/charging ones') with و 'and'. به دشت نبرد is a directional phrase 'into/toward the plain of battle' (به + place + نبرد 'battle'), describing their rush into the battlefield.
segments.s285-g125: برآمد is a past verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز + لشکرگه gives source 'from the army-camp'. آوای کوس is a genitive/construct: آوا ('sound/voice') of کوس ('the war-drum'), so 'the drum's sound rose from the camp.'
segments.s285-g126: همی again marks a continuing or iterative action ('kept/continually'). گرد here functions adverbially ('all around/about') modifying داد ('gave/sent'); بر آسمان is the target ('upon the sky'). داد بوس is a poetic collocation literally 'gave kisses' (بوس as noun), so the clause is a figurative description: the sound kept throwing/sending kisses upon the heavens.
segments.s285-g127: بزرگان (the great men/notables) is the subject; بر here functions as a preposition 'to/toward' in the sense 'came to'. پهلوان is the goal/recipient ('to the hero'), and آمدند is the past, 3rd-person plural verb 'they came'. This is straightforward subject–verb–object/goal order with a poetic preposition.
segments.s285-g128: پر از is an adjectival construction meaning 'full of' (پر 'full' + از 'of'), followed by خنده و شادمان ('laughter and gladness/rejoicing') describing the manner/state of those who arrived; آمدند repeats the past plural verb 'they came', emphasizing their joyful arrival.
segments.s285-g161: This nominal phrase describes 'his army' and uses the prepositional phrase 'آسوده از رنج و تاب' as a predicative adjective: 'آسوده' (relieved/eased) takes the preposition 'از' + nouns 'رنج و تاب' ('suffering/strain and weariness/endurance'). Word order is typical Persian: head noun سپاه + possessive/pronoun وی. The conjunction و simply links the two nouns رنج and تاب.
segments.s285-g162: A verbless clause in appearance but built around the perfect/stative participle بمانده 'remained/has remained' followed by the subject سپاهم ('my army'); Persian often places the predicate before the subject for emphasis. چنین 'thus/so' modifies the predicate, and در شتاب 'in haste' is a locative/adverbial phrase describing the manner.
segments.s285-g163: ولیکن 'but/yet' introduces a contrast; چنین 'such/so' qualifies امید 'hope'. دارم is the present-tense first-person singular of داشتن 'to have' (I have), so the clause literally reads 'yet I have such hope.' The pronoun من is the explicit subject/emphatic 'I'.
segments.s285-g164: که here is a complementizer 'that' introducing the content of the hope. آید is the present/subjunctive form 'will come/comes'. جهاندار is an epithet 'world-ruler/sovereign' modifying خورشید 'sun' (a metaphorical royal epithet), and من after خورشید functions as a possessive 'my' (the literal word is 'I', but syntactically marks possession).
segments.s285-g165: بیفروزد is a poetic verb meaning 'will illumine/kindle' (future/optative sense in context). The direct object این رزمگه را ('this battlefield' + definite object marker را) follows the verb for emphasis (word order is flexible in classical Persian). به فر ('with/by glory') expresses the instrument or means—'by/with (royal) glory.'
segments.s285-g166: This parallel clause continues the prophetic tone: بیارد is here a verb meaning 'will bring/bring about' (the given lemma senses do not match this use, so marked below). سپاهی is the object 'an army' (noun form), and به نو کینه‌ور is an adverbial phrase 'newly / fresh and vindictive' describing the army's disposition. The preposition به introduces that adverbial quality.
segments.s285-g167: یکی here is an indefinite determiner 'a certain' / 'someone' introducing هوشمندی as a substantive (a wise person). فرستاده‌ام is perfect tense 'I have sent' (first-person singular). In classical usage abstract nouns like هوشمندی can stand for a person possessing that quality.
segments.s285-g168: بسی is an adverb/adjective meaning 'many/much' here (the provided senses do not exactly match, so indicated below). شاه را uses را as the definite object marker attaching to the king, and پندها 'advices/counsels' is plural (object) with داده‌ام 'I have given' completing the clause in the perfect tense.
segments.s285-g73: بینداخت is a past tense verb meaning 'he threw/ hurled' (bi- prefix + انداخت). خنجر is its direct object 'dagger'. به کردار تیر is a prepositional simile: به + کردار ('in the manner/way of') + تیر ('arrow'), so the whole phrase means 'as if an arrow' or 'like an arrow'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: verb + object + simile phrase.
segments.s285-g74: بیامد is a simple past 'it came' or 'it reached' describing the dagger/shot; به marks the target 'to/at' and governs بازوی 'arm'. سالار پیر is a noun + epithet: سالار 'commander/chief' modified by پیر 'old/elderly'. The clause therefore says the missile reached/struck the old commander's arm.
segments.s285-g75: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'. گودرز is the subject. شد خسته is the periphrastic past 'became tired/was exhausted' (شد + adjective). بر دست اوی is an idiomatic agentive/locative phrase: بر + دست + اوی ('at/by his hand' or 'because of his hand/his action'); اوی is the possessive 'his'. The clause means 'when Goudarz became exhausted by his hand/actions.'
segments.s285-g76: ز here is the classical 'from/because of'. کینه 'grudge/hatred' is the cause. به خشم 'to/into anger' uses به to mark the resultant state. اندر آورد is a compound meaning 'brought into/turned into' (اندر + آورد, with آورد used in the sense 'cause/bring about'), and روی 'face' means 'expression/visage'—together 'he turned his face into anger' or 'his expression became angry.'
segments.s285-g77: بینداخت again 'he threw/ cast'. ژوپین is a padded jacket/gambeson (armor garment) and is the thrown object. به + پیران 'to/at the elders' (پیران plural of پیر) shows the target/recipient. رسید here simply reports that the thrown item 'reached' them.
segments.s285-g78: زره 'armor' is the subject/topic. بر تنش is a locative phrase 'on his body' (بر = on/upon, تن = body, ش = his). سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning 'completely/utterly' or 'all over.' بردرید is a past transitive 'tore apart/ cut to pieces.' Together: 'the armor on his body was torn apart entirely.'
segments.s285-g79: ز is the poetic 'from'. پشت 'the back' indicates the direction 'from behind.' اندر آمد is 'entered/approached' (came in), and به راه جگر is a locative/goal 'toward the path of the jigar'—jگر here means 'heart/seat of feeling' (vitals) rather than the organ alone. The clause describes an attack coming from the rear toward the vitals.
segments.s285-g80: بغرید is a past intransitive 'he roared/bellowed'. و joins the next phrase. آسیمه is an adjective/adverb meaning 'distraught, frantic' and modifies برگشت ('returned/turned back'); برگشت here means 'he turned/turned away' and is completed by سر 'his head' — 'he roared and, distraught, turned his head back.'
segments.s285-g81: برآمدش is the past intransitive verb برآمد with the enclitic -ش ('to/for him/it'), literally 'rose up for him.' خون جگر is a genitive-like compound 'blood of the liver' (an idiomatic way to name internal/bloody discharge here) and بر دهان uses بر + دهان to indicate motion toward or into the mouth. Word order is verb + object/enclitic then the noun phrase that specifies what rose.
segments.s285-g82: روانش is 'his soul/spirit' with possessive enclitic -ش; برآمد repeats the same verb 'rose/issued forth' indicating departure of the spirit. هم here means 'also/too,' and اندر زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that moment' or 'within the time.' The whole clause expresses that his soul likewise rose away at the same instant.
segments.s285-g83: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' شیر ژیان is a compound where ژیان (literally 'life') qualifies شیر, producing 'the living lion' (an epithet). اندر آمد is the older-style verbal expression 'came in/entered,' and به سر completes the motion idiomatically as 'came to the head' (a locative/figurative complement). This whole clause sets the time/context for the following verb.
segments.s285-g84: بنالید is the past tense 'he moaned/wailed.' با here introduces the addressee or accompaniment and is followed by داور دادگر, a noun+adjective phrase 'the just judge.' In older/poetic Persian با can mean 'to' or 'before' in the sense of addressing someone, so the clause means 'he lamented to the just judge.'
segments.s285-g85: بر آن کوه خارا places the action 'on that flinty mountain' (بر + demonstrative + noun + adjective). زمانی is an adverb meaning 'for a while' or 'for a time,' and تپید is the past intransitive 'it/he beat/throbbed/pounded.' The subject is not overtly marked (a common poetic feature), so the verb stands with the locative and temporal adjuncts to indicate that a beating/throbbing occurred there for a while.
segments.s285-g86: پس از is the compound preposition 'after (the occurrence of),' followed by کین 'vengeance' and the conjunction و linking آوردگاه 'battlefield.' آرمید is the past 'rested/was at rest.' The construction means 'after the vengeance, the battlefield rested' — verb at the end follows the classical Persian order with the temporal/causal phrase first.
segments.s285-g87: زمانه is used as 'time' or 'fortune' (the power of circumstance). به زهر آب places 'to/for poison the water' — here word order is poetic (preposition + modifier + noun). دادست is an archaic perfect/present meaning 'has given' or 'gave,' so the clause says that time has given poison to the water, with چنگ ('claw') left as the next noun that will act on what follows.
segments.s285-g88: بدرد here carries the sense 'to cause pain / to tear at' and takes دل شیر و چرم پلنگ as its object phrase: 'the heart of the lion and the hide of the leopard.' The phrase is a compact poetic clause meaning the claw (introduced in the previous segment) rends/gnaws at the lion's heart and the leopard's skin; note that possessor (شیر/پلنگ) follows the possessed (دل/چرم), as is common in older Persian poetry.
segments.s285-g89: چنین است is a copular phrase 'thus is'. خود is an intensifier 'the very/itself' placed before the noun phrase. گردش روزگار is a genitive/compound noun 'the turning/circuit of (the) days/fate' (گردش modifies روزگار). The whole segment states a general truth about the nature of time/fate.
segments.s285-g90: نگیرد is a negative present-form of گرفتن used here with the sense 'does not take/accept' or 'does not heed'; همی is an archaic/poetic continuous/emphatic particle 'ever/always' that follows the verb. پند آموزگار is the object noun phrase 'the advice of the teacher' (پند = advice; آموزگار = teacher). The subject (the 'turn of fate') is implied from the previous segment.
segments.s285-g91: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. گودرز is the subject. بر شد is a verbal phrase where the preposition بر plus شد conveys 'went up/ascended onto'; classical Persian often uses such combinations to mean 'mounted' or 'ascended'. بر آن کوهسار is the prepositional phrase 'onto that mountainside.' Word order is 'when + subject + verb + PP'.
segments.s285-g92: بدیدش is past 'saw him' with the clitic -ش marking the third-person object. بر آن‌گونه = 'in that manner/so', modifying the participial phrase افگنده خوار. افگنده is a past-participle/adjectival form 'cast/lying/strewn' and خوار is an adjective 'despised/humbled' describing the person seen. The clause reports what he saw.
segments.s285-g93: This is a series of coordinated noun phrases describing injuries. دریده is a past-participle/adjective 'torn' modifying دل و دست ('heart and hand'). و links items; بر خاک سر is a prepositional phrase 'and (with) head on the ground' (بر = on, خاک = earth/ground, سر = head). The copula is omitted as is normal in descriptive poetic lines.
segments.s285-g94: شکسته and گسسته are past-participles/adjectives 'broken' applied to the following nouns. سلیح = 'weapon' and کمر = 'waist/back' are linked by و. Adjective-before-noun order is frequent in poetry; the second adjective parallels the first to describe the warrior's ruined condition.
segments.s285-g95: چنین گفت گودرز is the narrative 'thus said Goudarz' (گفت = 'said' in past narrative). کای is a vocative particle 'O', and نره شیر is an epithet 'manly lion' (نره = manly/male, شیر = lion) addressing someone proudly. Note: the senses list provided for گفت does not include 'said', so that entry is marked below as not matching the provided senses.
segments.s285-g96: This is a pair of epithets in apposition to the addressee: سر پهلوانان uses سر as 'head/chief' of پهلوانان 'champions'. و links the second epithet. گرد دلیر is another title; گرد here functions as an honorific/epithet whose precise sense is not present in the provided sense list, while دلیر means 'brave'. The structure lists titles in parallel.
segments.s285-g33: همی شد بر آن کوهسر بر دوان = a compact verbal phrase: همی شد gives a continuous/incipient sense ('began/kept on'), followed by the directional phrase بر آن کوهسر ('onto that mountain‑summit') and بر دوان where بر marks motion/toward and دوان is the verbal stem 'running/rush' — together: 'he ran up onto that summit.' Note the classical order: verbal phrase before or surrounding the subject (subject may be implied or follow).
segments.s285-g34: کزو بازگردد مگر پهلوان = a relative/adverbial clause indicating source and expectation: کزو 'from which', بازگردد 'might/should return' (subjunctive/optative sense of بازگشت), مگر here functions as a speculative or concessive particle ('perhaps' / rhetorical), and پهلوان is the subject 'the champion'. The clause modifies the preceding motion to the summit: 'from which perhaps the hero will return.'
segments.s285-g35: نگه کرد گودرز و بگریست زار = compound/light‑verb construction نگه + کرد ('made a look' = 'looked') with the proper name گودرز appearing after the verb for emphasis; و links the coordinated action; بگریست is simple past 'he wept' and زار is an adverb/adjective 'wretchedly' or 'bitterly' modifying the weeping.
segments.s285-g36: بترسید از گردش روزگار = past indicative بترسید ('he feared') takes the prepositional object introduced by از; گردش روزگار is a noun‑noun compound meaning 'the turning/rotation of time' i.e. 'the vicissitudes of fate' (گردش = turn, روزگار = fate/times).
segments.s285-g37: بدانست کش نیست با کس وفا = بدانست 'he realized' introduces the following clause. کش نیست functions as an archaic negative existential ('there is no ...'); با کس وفا 'fidelity with anyone' is the clausal complement, literally 'there is no fidelity with anyone.' Word order places the verb of cognition before the clause it governs, typical in classical narrative.
segments.s285-g38: میان بسته دارد ز بهر جفا = a verbless/state clause rendered with دارد 'has/keeps': میان بسته دارد 'he/they keep the middle (i.e. relations) closed/tied' (میان = between/relations, بسته = bound/closed), followed by a purpose/reason phrase ز بهر جفا 'for the sake of cruelty/injustice'. In narrative this describes a continuing state — 'keeps relations closed out of cruelty.'
segments.s285-g39: فغان کرد کای نامور پهلوان = فغان کرد 'he lamented/cried aloud' with کای a vocative ('O') addressing نامور پهلوان 'renowned champion.' The vocative particle directly precedes the epithet; this is a conventional gesture of address in epic speech.
segments.s285-g40: چه بودت که ایدون پیاده دوان = a rhetorical question: چه بودت که 'what was it that' (بودت = past 2nd‑sing. 'you were / you had'), introducing a causal clause with که; ایدون here means 'to this point / hitherto' (literally 'came/come'), and پیاده دوان 'you ran on foot' (پیاده 'on foot', دوان 'ran'). Overall: 'What was it that caused you to run on foot up to here?'.
segments.s285-g65: همی + گشت is a habitual/continuous past construction: همی marks repeated or ongoing action and گشت (past of گشتن) means 'went/roamed'. The subject گودرز follows the verb (a poetic/inverted word order). بر گرد کوه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'around the mountain' (گرد = 'around').
segments.s285-g66: نبودش is the negative past 'there was not' with the clitic -ش referring forward to the thing missing; بدو ('to him') marks the beneficiary/dative, so نبودش بدو راه = 'there was no way/path for him'. و آمد ستوه is coordinate: آمد (past of آمدن) here = 'he became' or 'he came to be' and ستوه is a predicative adjective 'weary'.
segments.s285-g67: پیاده ببود uses the archaic past/copular form ببود (archaic variant of بود) to state 'he was on foot'; the adjective/phrase پیاده precedes the verb. و سپر برگرفت joins the next action: برگرفت (بر + گرفت) is a past action 'took up/raised' (سپر = shield).
segments.s285-g68: چو = 'as/like' introduces a comparison. نخچیربانان is a plural noun 'herdsmen/keepers'; که is a relative pronoun 'who/that'; اندر گرفت = 'took/ seized within' (اندر = 'in/within/among', گرفت = past of گرفتن 'seized/took'). The clause compares his action to that of the herdsmen.
segments.s285-g69: گرفته is a perfect/participial form 'having taken' or 'grasping' modifying the subject; سپر پیش = 'shield in front' (پیش = 'in front/forward'). و links to ژوپین به دست, where ژوپین is 'gambeson/padded jacket' and به دست is an instrumental/locative phrase meaning 'in hand' (به here marks the instrument/place).
segments.s285-g70: به بالا نهاده is a compound where به بالا ('upwards') + نهاده (past participle of نهادن 'placed/put') conveys 'having lifted/raised upward'. سر از جای پست = 'his head from the low place' (سر = head, از = 'from', جای پست = 'low place/low position'): overall 'he raised his head up from the low spot.'
segments.s285-g71: همی دید is the narrative past 'saw' with همی giving a continuous or vivid tone. پیران is the plural subject 'the elders' (verb–subject inversion is poetic). مر is an archaic/clitic dative 'to me' (used here in the line; it stands before the object او را — او را is the definite object 'him') and ز دور = 'from afar'. The sequence literally places the dative clitic unusually but functions as 'the elders saw him from afar (for me).'
segments.s285-g72: بجست is past of جستن and here best read as 'leapt/sprung up' (physical action). از بر is a poetic prepositional bundle 'from beside/of' (از = from, بر = on/at), followed by سنگ سالار تور — 'the rock/stone of the commander Tur' (سالار تور = 'commander Tur' in genitive relation to سنگ). Word order places the locator after the verb as is common in poetry.
segments.s285-g129: چنین گفت لشکر is a subject-verb phrase in which the adverbial 'چنین' (thus) precedes the verb. 'لشکر' (the host/army) is the subject after the verb, a common classical word order where the verb phrase can come before or around the subject; 'گفت' is the past verb 'said'. 'مگر' begins a contrasting clause that follows this utterance.
segments.s285-g130: از او بازگردید تیره روان is a short clause describing action and state. 'از او بازگردید' is a past action phrase (بازگردید = turned back/returned from him), where 'از' marks separation, 'او' is the third‑person pronoun. 'تیره روان' is a descriptive compound (together meaning 'dark‑spirited' or 'with a dark soul') that follows the verb and characterizes the subject (the host/champion) in classical Persian syntax.
segments.s285-g131: که پیران یکی شیردل مرد بود is a relative/explanatory clause introduced by 'که' (= 'that/for'), describing the elders. Word order places the group ('پیران' elders) then 'یکی' (one of them) and the predicate 'شیردل مرد بود' (was a lion‑hearted man). 'بود' is the past copula here (was).
segments.s285-g132: همه‌ساله جویای آورد بود is a time‑adverbial plus verbal predicate: 'همه‌ساله' (yearly) sets the habitual frame; 'جویای' (seeking) + infinitive/stem 'آورد' (to bring) form a verbal phrase, and 'بود' here functions as an auxiliary to indicate a repeated/past habitual action ('used to seek to bring').
segments.s285-g133: چنین یاد کرد آن زمان پهلوان begins with 'چنین' (thus), then the verbal phrase 'یاد کرد' (literally 'made mention' / 'recalled'); 'آن زمان' (that time) is a temporal phrase; 'پهلوان' (the hero) appears as the topic/agent. The structure is classical: adverbial + verb + time + subject/topic.
segments.s285-g134: سپرده بدو گوش پیر و جوان is a compact clause where 'سپرده' (entrusted / entrusted thing) functions as the past participle/noun meaning 'entrusted (to someone)'; 'بدو' = 'to him'; 'گوش' here means 'ear' in the idiomatic sense 'attention/listening'; 'پیر و جوان' ('old and young') is the scope of that entrusted attention. Word order: object/participial noun + indirect object + thing entrusted + beneficiaries.
segments.s285-g135: به انگشت بنمود جای نبرد: 'به انگشت' is an instrumental use (by/with the finger) marked by 'به' + noun; 'بنمود' is the past form of the verb 'نماياندن/نمودن' meaning 'showed/pointed'; 'جای نبرد' is the noun phrase 'place of battle' (جای = place, نبرد = battle). Classical Persian permits this compact sequence: instrument → verb → object phrase.
segments.s285-g136: بگفت آنک با او زمانه چه کرد is a reported‑speech clause: 'بگفت' (he said), followed by 'آنک' (= 'that which/see that'), then the content 'با او زمانه چه کرد' ('what time/fate has done to him'), where 'با' marks the relation 'to/with (him)', 'زمانه' = 'time/fortune' (here 'fate/the times'), and 'کرد' is the past verb 'did'.
segments.s285-g137: به رهام (preposition + name) = 'to Roham' (recipient). فرمود is a literary past-tense verb here meaning 'he commanded/said' (polite/literary use). تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that / in order that') and is followed by برنشست, a past form meaning 'he sat up / mounted' (بر- prefix + نشست, literary order). The whole segment reads: 'He commanded to Roham that he should mount.'
segments.s285-g138: به آوردن او is a gerundial phrase: به + آوردن ('by/when bringing') with او as its object 'him'. میان را is a definite object phrase 'the middle / the interval' with را marking it. ببست is a past tense verb 'he closed / he shut / he bound' (literary form). Together it means 'on bringing him, he closed the middle (i.e. closed the gap between them).' Note the causal/time link: the gerundial phrase sets the circumstance for the main verb ببست.
segments.s285-g139: بدو is a prepositional pronoun meaning 'to him' (indirect object). گفت is the past tense 'said' (here introducing direct speech/imperative). او را is the direct object 'him' with را marking definiteness/emphasis. به زین 'to the saddle' is the goal/attachment, and برببند is an imperative form meaning 'bind on / fasten (him)' (literary/poetic imperative). Word order is typical Persian: indirect object + گفت + direct object + imperative clause.
segments.s285-g140: بیاور is an imperative 'bring (him)'. چنان = 'such / thus' qualifies the manner. تازیان here is used in a horsemanship/epic sense (i.e. Arab coursers / Arabian horses) rather than simply 'Arabs' (poetic trope). بر is the preposition 'on/upon', and نوند appears as a poetic name or reference (likely a proper name or traditional epithet—here treated as a target/location/horse-name). The clause issues another command simile: 'bring him in such manner as the Taziān on Nāvand.'
segments.s285-g141: درفش و سلیحش is a coordinated noun phrase 'his banner and his weapon(s)' (سلیح + possessive suffix -ش). چنان هم که هست is an idiomatic clause 'just as it is / in that very state' (چنان = 'so/thus', هم adding emphasis, که هست = 'that is'). The clause instructs to leave the banner and arms unchanged.
segments.s285-g142: به درع و میانش = 'to/onto his shield/armor and his middle/chest' (میانش = میان + possessive -ش). مبر is a negative imperative form 'do not take/do not lay' (literary), while هیچ دست = 'any hand' (هیچ = any/none; دست = hand/power). The literal instruction is 'do not put any hand on his armor or his chest'—i.e. do not touch them.
segments.s285-g143: بر آن گونه = 'in that manner / in that way' (بر + demonstrative آن + گونه 'manner'). چون پهلوان = 'as a champion / like a hero' (چون introduces a comparison). کرد یاد is the verb + noun construction where کرد (past 'did/made') takes یاد ('remembrance/recalling') as its object, here meaning 'having recalled/remembering'. Grammatically this is a subordinate adverbial: 'recalling/remembering as the hero had done.'
segments.s285-g144: برون is an adverb 'out / forth'. تاخت is the past tense verb 'galloped / rushed' (third-person). رهام is the subject (proper name). چون تندباد is a simile 'like a gale' (چون = like, تندباد = storm/gale). The clause is a simple past action: 'Roham rushed forth like a gale.'
segments.s285-g169: که‌گر is an archaic conditional particle (= که گر) meaning "if." The clause that follows names the subject and its object: شاه (the king) is subject; ترکان modifies/identifies the army (an attribute of سپاه) — the poet uses a somewhat inverted, poetic word order so that "ترکان" and "سپاه" surround the verb. بیارد is the archaic 3rd-person form meaning "brings/should bring" (a present/subjunctive-like form of آوردن). سپاه is the noun "army/host."
segments.s285-g170: نداریم is present indicative (1st plural) meaning "we do not have / we have not"; here it expresses lack of possession/possession of a footing. پای is used figuratively as "foot / base / foothold." اندر is an older preposition meaning "in/within." این is the demonstrative "this." کینه‌گاه is a compound noun "place/seat of hatred/vengeance." Together: "we have no foothold in this place of hatred."
segments.s285-g171: گمانم is گمان + enclitic -م = "my belief/guess." چنان است = "it is thus/so." کو here is an archaic relative/introducer meaning roughly "who/that" (poetic usage introducing the subordinate clause). با سپاه is a prepositional phrase "with an army" where سپاه is the object of با. The whole segment sets up a content clause: "my guess is that (the one) who with an army..."
segments.s285-g172: به یاری بیاید is a verbal phrase: به یاری = "to help / for help," and بیاید is the 3rd-person (subjunctive/optative/poetic present) meaning "comes / may come / will come." The following بدین is the fused form of به + این (= "to/into this") and is here split in the edition; رزمگاه = "battlefield/place of combat." So the clause reads "(whoever) comes with an army to help to this battlefield."
segments.s285-g173: مر is an archaic dative/ethical marker meaning "to me / for me." این کشتگان را = "these slain" with را marking the definite direct object. بر این دشت کین = "on this plain of vengeance" (بر = "on/upon," دشت = "plain," کین = "vengeance/hatred"). The sequence directs where these slain are to be set.
segments.s285-g174: چنین هم = "thus/so likewise." بدارید is the present imperative/plural of داشتن used here with the sense "keep / hold / place" (an imperative instruction). بر پشت زین = a locative phrase "on the back of the saddle" (بر = "on," پشت = "back," زین = "saddle"). So: "also keep them like this on the saddle's back."
segments.s285-g175: کز is a poetic preposition meaning "from which / because of / that which," here introducing a causal clause. این کشتگان = "these slain." جان ما = "our life/heart/soul" (جان used for inner life or feeling). بیغم است is a poetic adjective meaning "is without grief / free from sorrow" (بیغم = بی‌غم). Together: "Because of these slain, our hearts/lives are without sorrow."
segments.s285-g176: روان here means "the spirit/soul" (often used for a dead or exalted person's soul). سیاووش is the proper name Siavash. ز = "from/because of" (poetic preposition), این = "this," and خرم = "joyful/glad." است is the copula "is." The clause says: "Siavash's soul is joyful because of this."
segments.s285-g153: 'که‌ای' here functions as a vocative particle ('O ...') addressing someone; 'نامور' is an epithet 'renowned'; 'پشت ایران سپاه' is a genitive phrase where پشت (literally 'back') is used metaphorically as 'support/protector' and is followed by its possessor 'ایران سپاه' ('the host of Iran')—poetic word order places modifiers before/after more freely.
segments.s285-g154: This is an optative clause: 'باد' is the optative particle meaning 'may (it be)', 'پرستنده تخت تو' is a nominal predicate 'a worshipper/devotee of your throne' (تخت تو = 'your throne'), and 'ماه' is the grammatical subject placed at the end for emphasis. The whole order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: 'May the moon be a worshipper of your throne.'
segments.s285-g155: This is a past/perfect construction: 'کرده‌ای' is 2nd-person perfect 'you have made'; 'فدای سپه' ('a sacrifice for the host') is the object/complement of کرده‌ای, and 'جان و تن' are the things sacrificed (placed at the end for emphasis). Poetic inversion places the verbal phrase before the direct objects.
segments.s285-g156: 'به' is a preposition here ('in/into' or 'to'); 'پیری' means 'old age' and is qualified by the following apposition 'زمان روزگار کهن' ('the time/age of ancient days')—a stacked modifier where زمان and روزگار both mean 'time/age' and کهن 'ancient' characterizes the era. The phrase as a whole describes the temporal setting or consequence ('in the old age of ancient times').
segments.s285-g157: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus said' (standard formulaic opening); 'گودرز' is the speaker's name; 'با مهتران' means 'with the chiefs/elders' (مهتران = chief/leader), so the whole chunk is 'Thus Goudarz said with the chiefs'—word order is conventional for reported speech in epic Persian.
segments.s285-g158: This is a subordinate clause introduced by 'که' ('that'); 'چون' here = 'since/because', marking a causal clause; 'رزم ما گشت' places the verb 'گشت' (past 'became/turned') after subject 'رزم ما' ('our battle'); 'ز' marks cause or source ('because of/from'), 'این‌سان' = 'thus/so', and 'گران' is a predicate adjective 'heavy/costly/grievous'. So: 'that, since our battle has become thus grievous.'
segments.s285-g159: مرا در دل آید که is an idiomatic verbless-experiencer construction: literally 'to me in (the) heart comes that...'. مرا is the object-form pronoun marking the experiencer ('to me'), در دل is a locative phrase ('in the heart/mind'), and آید is a 3rd-person singular present of آمد meaning 'comes' (here used impersonally: 'it occurs to me'). که introduces the subordinate clause whose subject follows (افراسیاب). Note the word order is object/locative + verb + conjunction + subject of the following clause, typical of classical Persian idiom.
segments.s285-g160: افراسیاب سپه بگذراند بدین روی آب is the content clause introduced by که. افراسیاب is the agent; سپه is the entity being caused to move (direct object) and بگذراند is the 3rd-person singular present/causative form of گذراند (bi‑prefix + گذراند) meaning 'cause to pass/lead across'. بدین is an archaic/poetic contraction of به + این 'by/with this / in this way', and روی آب is an adverbial phrase 'on/over the water'. Together: 'Afrasiab will cause the army to pass over the water in this way.' Note that the object-marker را is omitted, as is common in poetry.
segments.s285-g1: سپهدار is a title/noun meaning ‘commander’ and heads the noun phrase. ایران و توران are the two lands connected by و (‘and’) following standard Persian order (noun—و—noun). The adjective دژم follows the nouns and qualifies them (postpositive adjective typical in Persian poetry) meaning ‘grim/sullen’.
segments.s285-g2: فراز آمدند is a past plural verb phrase: فراز (literally ‘up/on high’) plus آمدند (‘they came/rose’) gives the sense ‘they rose up’ or ‘they came forth’. اندر آن کین is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘into/among that enmity/hatred’ (آن = 'that', کین = 'hatred, vengeance'). به هم is a reciprocal expression meaning ‘at/against one another’ (به = toward/into, هم = together/each other), linking the action to mutual hostility.
segments.s285-g3: همی is a frequent classical particle marking continued or emphatic past action (here a habitual/continuous flavour). برنوشتند is the past plural of نوشتن (‘they wrote/inscribed/addressed’). هر دو زمین is the object phrase ‘both lands’ (هر دو = both, زمین = land/country); word order (verb then object) is common in poetic Persian.
segments.s285-g4: همه qualifies the following nouns distributively (‘every/everyone’ or ‘all’). دل پر از درد is a common Persian collocation: دل ‘heart’ + پر ‘full (of)’ + از درد ‘of/with pain’ meaning ‘heart full of sorrow’. و links the two clauses; سر پر ز کین parallels the previous construction with سر ‘head’ + پر ‘full’ + ز (poetic from/of) + کین ‘hatred/vengeance’ meaning ‘head full of hatred’. The adjective/phrase order (noun + پر + از/ز + noun) is idiomatic.
segments.s285-g5: به آوردگاه is a directional prepositional phrase ‘to the battlefield’ (به = to/into + آوردگاه = place of bringing / battlefield). سواران is the subject or topical noun ‘the horsemen/riders’. ز گرد uses ز (= from, poetic) + گرد here meaning ‘dust’ (the dust raised by the riders), forming an ablative phrase that links causally to the following clause.
segments.s285-g6: فروماند is a past/passive‑like verbal adjective meaning ‘was worn out/dimmed’ or ‘became exhausted/abated’. خورشید روز نبرد is a compound noun phrase ‘the sun of the day of battle’ (خورشید = sun, روز = day, نبرد = battle). The construction expresses that the sun of that battle‑day was dimmed/obscured (by the dust mentioned before).
segments.s285-g7: The repeated preposition به functions instrumentally here and is best read ‘with/by’ before each weapon: به تیغ و به خنجر به گرز و کمند = ‘with sword and with dagger, with mace and lasso’. The repetition is a poetic device enumerating instruments of combat; each noun names a weapon (تیغ, خنجر, گرز) or a snaring implement (کمند).
segments.s285-g8: ز هر گونه is a phrase meaning ‘of/from every kind’ (ز = from/poetic, هر گونه = every kind/type). برنهادند is past plural ‘they lifted up/brandished/raised’ and here applies to بند, so the phrase means they ‘raised/brandished snares or bonds’. بند in this context takes the sense of fetter/snare/cord — i.e., they employed snares or entangling implements of every sort.
segments.s285-g97: جهان (subject) + similes 'چون من و چون تو' ('like me and like you') come before the verbal predicate; دید is a past-tense perfective verb 'saw/has seen' and بسيار here quantifies what was seen ('many'). The clause literally reads 'The world has seen many like me and like you.'
segments.s285-g98: نخواهد is the classical negative-future auxiliary ('will not'); همی is the old Persian aspectual/emphatic particle (here: ongoing/ever/indeed); آرمید is a past-form verb used with خواهد to yield a future meaning ('rest'). با کسی = 'with anyone/with anyone at all'. Together: 'it will not ever rest with anyone.'
segments.s285-g99: چو here functions as a temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'; گودرز is the subject. دیدش is the past verb 'saw' with the clitic object pronoun -ش 'him' attached (V+obj). چنان + مرده‌خوار is a predicative nominal phrase describing what Goudarz saw: 'such a carrion-eater.'
segments.s285-g100: This phrase is an adjectival/adverbial description: 'به خاک و به خون' ('on/into the earth and into blood') fronting two parallel prepositional phrases; بر تپیده is a past participle-like description ('having been beaten / thrashing upon'), and به زار qualifies the state as sorrowful/woeful. The whole strings together as 'thrown on earth and in blood, wretchedly writhing.'
segments.s285-g101: فروبرد is a past transitive verb 'he sank/ thrust in'; its object is چنگال 'claw/talon' (accusative). The clause then continues with a coordinated verb phrase 'و خون برگرفت' — 'and he took up/raised blood' (برگرفت = past of 'take up'). The two verbs are coordinated actions performed by the same agent.
segments.s285-g102: بخورد is the past-tense verb 'he ate' (here a simple past action). The conjunction و links the next predicate. بیالود in context functions as a verbal/participial idea 'to smear/bedaub' (note: the printed lemma senses lean adjectival, but here it is used in an active/result sense—treated as 'stained/soiled'). روی‌ای شگفت is a noun phrase 'a wondrous/strange face' (روی = face, شگفت = wondrous/strange) that is the surface affected.
segments.s285-g103: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'; 'ز خون سیاوش' = 'from Siavash's blood'. خروشید is a past verb 'a roar/outcry rose' (an impersonal or intransitive occurrence). زار modifies the manner of that outcry ('lamenting/pitiable'), so the literal sense is 'a lamenting outcry rose from Siavash's blood.'
segments.s285-g104: نیایش 'prayer/supplication' is the object; همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle indicating ongoing or repeated action; کرد is the past verb 'made/did' (here 'offered' or 'made' a prayer). بر + کردگار = 'to/toward the Creator' (بر used with the sense 'toward/to'). Word order is object + aspect particle + verb + prepositional phrase.
segments.s285-g177: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). هم‌چنین is an adverb modifying آوریم meaning 'thus/like this'. نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of the king' (نزد + noun). آوریم is first-person plural present-subjunctive/optative used here in a conditional sense 'we bring' (literally 'if we bring ...'). The object ('him/them') is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s285-g178: شود is a third-person singular present-subjunctive/auxiliary used here to mean 'will become / will be' (future sense) with the understood subject 'he' (the king becomes pleased). شاد is the predicate adjective 'glad'. و joins the clauses. ز این پایگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from this stronghold/base' (ز = 'from', این پایگاه = 'this stronghold'), and آوریم repeats the first-person-plural verb 'we will bring/take (him) from' that place — آوریم again in the subjunctive/future sense bound to the conditional if-clause.
segments.s285-g179: که here introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that' / 'for'). آشوب is the head noun 'turmoil' and takes the following genitive/possessive group ترکان و ایرانیان ('of the Turks and Iranians') — i.e. 'the turmoil of the Turks and Iranians.' Word order is noun + genitive group (classical Persian style).
segments.s285-g180: از این بد is a prepositional phrase 'from / because of this bad/evil' (ز/از used poetically). کجا is a rhetorical interrogative 'where/anywhere'. کم شد is a compound verb: کم ('less, reduced') as predicative idea and شد ('became') as the auxiliary meaning 'was reduced / became less'. اندر میان is an adverbial phrase 'in the midst' / 'among them' (اندر + میان), giving the location for the reduction (i.e. it did not lessen in their midst).
segments.s285-g181: همه is the subject 'all / everyone'. یکسره is an adverb modifying خواندند meaning 'all together / at once / completely'. خواندند is simple past, 3rd-person plural 'they cried/called out'. آفرین is the interjection 'Bravo!' (the thing they called out).
segments.s285-g182: که introduces the content of their acclamation — here a wish/prayer ('that / may it be that ...'). بی‌تو = 'without you'. مبادا is an optative/jussive particle meaning 'may it not be' or 'let it not be' (negative wish). زمان و زمین are the items wished about ('time and earth'); the copular verb ('be' / نباشد) is omitted as typical in poetry, so the whole means 'may time and the earth never be without you.'
segments.s285-g183: همه 'all' + سودمندی 'goodness/benefit' form the noun phrase 'all benefit'. ز گفتار is a prepositional phrase 'from/of (your) speech/words'. تست in classical/poetic Persian is an archaic contracted form of the copula meaning 'it is yours / is of you' (i.e. 'is from you'); this sense is not represented in the provided sense list, so it is marked as unmatched. The whole clause says 'all benefit is from your speech.'
segments.s285-g184: خور و ماه 'the sun and the moon' is a compound subject. روشن is a predicate adjective 'bright/illuminated'. بدیدار تست is a compact poetic expression meaning 'by/at your sight or by your presence (they are bright)'; بدیدار reflects an old/poetic locution related to 'به دیدار' ('in/at the sight/presence of'), and تست again is the archaic copula 'is/are of you' (not represented in the provided senses). The clause therefore means 'the sun and moon are bright by your presence.'
segments.s285-g145: 'کشید' is the simple past (3rd person) of کشیدن meaning “to pull/drag” (here: they pulled/dragged). The prepositional phrase 'از بر' literally 'from off/upon' marks removal from the horse; word order in poetry places the noun phrase 'اسب روشن تنش' after that: 'اسب روشن' = 'the shining/bright horse' (noun + adjective) and 'تنش' = 'his body' (تن + enclitic possessive -ش). Together they mean 'they pulled his body off the shining horse.'
segments.s285-g146: The phrase is inverted for poetic effect: 'به خون اندرون غرقه' = 'within, drenched in blood' (به + noun + اندرون + adjective), followed by 'بد جوشنش' = 'his cuirass (جوشن) badly/ill (بد)'. The enclitic -ش on جوشن marks possession ('his armor'). The whole segment describes the armor's interior being soaked with blood.
segments.s285-g147: 'چنان' = 'thus/so' and 'هم' is an emphatic/adverbial 'also/even' (here reinforcing 'thus'). 'ببستش' is a past narrative form meaning 'they bound/tied him' (بستن + object enclitic -ش). 'به خم کمند' is a prepositional phrase 'into/with the curve (خم) of the lasso (کمند)'—'به' here indicates direction/into the loop.
segments.s285-g148: 'فرود آوریدش' is a compound verb (فرود + آوردن) meaning 'they brought (him) down' (past, 3rd pl; -ش = him). 'ز' (poetic form of از) = 'from', and 'کوه بلند' = 'high mountain' (noun + adjective). The whole line says they lowered/brought him down from the high mountain.
segments.s285-g149: 'چو' here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. 'درفشش' (درفش + enclitic ـش) = 'his standard/flag'; 'از جایگاه' = 'from its station/place' and 'نشان' = 'sign/token'. The phrase sets up 'when (they) saw/not saw the standard's sign at its post.' Word order is inverted for poetry: the standard is fronted and the temporal 'چو' introduces the clause.
segments.s285-g150: 'ندیدند' is past tense, 3rd person plural negative 'they did not see'. 'گردان گردنکشان' is a noun phrase 'the troop (گردان) of neck-raisers / proud ones (گردنکشان)' — i.e., the rebellious band — and in poetic word order the subject follows the verb. The object (the sign/standard) is implicit; the clause means that the troop did not see it.
segments.s285-g151: 'همه' = 'all/everyone'. 'خواندند' here means 'they cried/called out' (past, 3rd pl) and takes 'آفرین' (an interjection 'Bravo!') as its object. 'سر به سر' is an idiom meaning 'one after another' or 'in succession', so the phrase means 'all shouted “Bravo!” in turn.'
segments.s285-g152: 'ابر پهلوان' is a poetic epithet: ابر used here as a lofty/intensifying image (literally 'cloud') qualifying 'پهلوان' = 'champion/hero' — i.e., 'the great/famed champion'. 'زمین' = 'the land' (i.e., of the land) and the fixed idiom 'در به در' = 'door to door' or 'wandering from place to place'; together the segment names the great champion of the land, characterized by roaming 'door to door'.
segments.s285-g17: از آن پس is an adverbial phrase meaning “after that / from that time on” (از + demonstrative آن + پس). کمان و تیر are the coordinated objects and برگرفتند is the perfective past plural verb “they took up / lifted”; word order is somewhat poetic (object before verb and the second object placed after the verb).
segments.s285-g18: This is an appositive nominal phrase identifying the doers:سالارِ لشکر “commanders of the army/host.” The doubled دو (two … two …) is a poetic piling of numerals with two epithets (هشیار، پیر) placed after the noun; modifiers follow the head in classical Persian.
segments.s285-g19: یکی here is an indefinite/partitive “one (of them).” تیرباران گرفتند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: گرفتند (past plural) with تیرباران as its object/description — “they made/let loose a shower of arrows.” سخت is an adverb “firmly/strongly / fiercely.”
segments.s285-g20: چو introduces a simile “as/like.” باد خزان is a genitive phrase “the autumn wind.” بر here means “upon/on”; جهد is a poetic noun “effort/striving” used figuratively (the wind’s buffeting), and بر درخت literally “upon the tree.” The whole phrase compares the arrow-shower to the autumn wind working upon a tree.
segments.s285-g21: نگه کرد is a compound with نگه (gaze/keeping) + کرد (past “did”) = “he looked / kept watch.” The proper name گودرز is the subject; تیر خدنگ is the object phrase (tīr + epithet خدنگ = a dagger‑like or barbed arrow). Classical Persian often places the verbal nucleus (کرد) next to the verbal noun and the subject after it.
segments.s285-g22: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause modifying تیر خدنگ. آهن ندارد is a present/historic‑present negated predicate “it has no iron.” مر او را is an archaic dative/possessive construction (poetic clitic) roughly “for it/to it / for him” with او as the pronoun and را the object marker; نه سنگ negates the noun “nor stone.” Overall: “which has neither iron nor (even) stone (for its head).”
segments.s285-g23: به + برگستوان (literary/poetic compound for the shoulder/arm) marks the target of the action: “at/on the arm.” برزد is an archaic past form meaning here “struck / smote / fell upon” and بردرید is a past verb “he tore apart / cut off.” The sequence gives two quick past actions (strike → sever).
segments.s285-g24: تگاور is a common epic term for a fighting horseman/rider; بلرزید is past tense “trembled / shuddered.” دم is “breath / moment,” and درکشید (here) means “drew in” or “took a breath” (a sudden inhalation). The two verbs are coordinated: the rider quivered and gasped.
segments.s285-g9: فراز آمد is a two-word poetic phrase: فراز here functions adverbially ('on high, aloft') and آمد is the past-tense verb 'came/arrived'. آن گردش ایزدی is a noun phrase following the verb in elevated word order: آن = 'that', گردش = 'turn/turning (of fate)', ایزدی = adjectival 'divine'.
segments.s285-g10: This clause has straightforward prepositional motion: از ایران 'from Iran' followed by به توران 'to Turan', and رسید is the past verb 'reached/arrived'. آن بدی is a nominative phrase ('that misfortune/evil'), placed after the verb in poetic order.
segments.s285-g11: ابا is an archaic verb meaning 'refused' or 'was refused' (here best taken impersonally); خواست is used as 'will/desire', and یزدانش is یزدان + -ش (third-person possessive suffix) = 'his God' or 'God of him'. چاره نماند is a nominal predicate 'no remedy remained' with نماند in the past tense meaning 'was left/ remained'.
segments.s285-g12: کرا is an archaic relative/pronominal word meaning 'for whom' or 'who', introducing the affected person; کوشش و زور و یاره is a tricolon listing 'effort, strength, and companions' (یاره = followers/assistants), and نماند repeats the past 'were not left/ remained'. Word order puts the person (کرا) up front for emphasis.
segments.s285-g13: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction meaning 'kept watch' or 'looked' (نگه = gaze/watch + کرد = did). پیران 'the elders' follows the verb in the poem's flexible word order. که introduces the indirect question هنگام چیست 'what the moment/time is' (هنگام = 'time/moment', چی = 'what', ست = copula 'is').
segments.s285-g14: بدانست is the past-tense verb 'they knew it' (بد- intensifying prefix + دانست 'knew'). کان is an archaic particle here functioning like 'that/this' pointing to the situation just asked about, followed by گردش ایزدیست 'is a divine turning' (گردش = 'turn/turning', ایزدیست = 'is divine' — a predicative adjective with the old copular ending).
segments.s285-g15: ولیکن is a conjunctive 'but/yet'. به مردی literally 'to/for a man' here introduces the actor in a poetically inverted way (can imply 'a man' or 'with manliness'); همی is the archaic continual particle marking ongoing action ('still/continually'), کرد is the past verb 'did', and کار is the object 'work/task'.
segments.s285-g16: بکوشید is the past tense of the verb 'to strive/try' (ب- prefixed classical form). با here is the preposition 'with', گردش روزگار is a genitive-like noun phrase 'the turning/course of time' where روزگار carries the meaning 'fate/times', so the whole phrase expresses striving in relation to (or against) the course/turning of fate.
segments.s285-g25: This clause uses the simple past verb بیفتاد (“he fell”), conjunction و linking the next action, then the name پیران with the verb درآمد used intransitively as “came/entered.” In classical Persian the verb can follow the subject or be implied; here پیران (proper name) is the actor who “came beneath” (به + زیر). به + زیر together indicate movement into a position under someone.
segments.s285-g26: بغلتید is a past action meaning “embraced/laid hold of” (here singular agent). زیرش (= زیر + -ش, ‘beneath him’) is the locative object indicating where the action occurred, and سوار دلیر is an appositive noun phrase (‘the brave rider/horseman’) identifying the agent; Persian poetry often places the verb before or adjacent to the subject for effect.
segments.s285-g27: بدانست is a past-tense compound meaning “he knew/realized.” کآمد is an archaic contracted form equivalent to modern که آمد (“that had come/that came”); زمانه is the noun “time/age” and فراز here qualifies it as having risen or reached a high point—together meaning “he perceived that the moment/time had arrived.”
segments.s285-g28: وز here functions poetically as a fused particle meaning roughly ‘and from’ (classical poetic contraction of و + ز). آن روز تیره = “that dark/ominous day.” نیابد is a negative-present/subjunctive-like form meaning “will not find/there will not be found,” and جواز means “permission/justification,” so the clause states that from that dark day no justification/escape will be found.
segments.s285-g29: This is an ergative/causal expression: ز نیرو = “by/through force” (ز here = ‘from/by’ in poetic usage), به دو نیم شد = “was made into two halves” (passive/resultative: ‘was split in two’), and دست راست = “the right hand.” Word order places instrument/cause first, then the result.
segments.s285-g30: هم آنگه (“also/just so; then likewise”) links this action to the previous scene. بغلتید again = “he embraced/encircled,” و = “and,” بر پای خاست = a fixed classical phrase meaning “rose to his feet” (بر + پای = onto the feet, خاست = rose). The verbs are past, and the clause describes a quick recovery/action after injury.
segments.s285-g31: ز here is the poetic preposition ‘from,’ so ز گودرز بگریخت = ‘he fled from Goudarz.’ شد سوی کوه = ‘he went/turned toward the mountain’ (شد used as an intransitive past auxiliary/verb meaning ‘went/became’ plus سوی = ‘toward’). The clause presents rapid movement away from the named person/place.
segments.s285-g32: غمی شد = ‘he became sorrowful’ (شد = became). ز درد دویدن ستوه = ‘weary from the pain of running’: ز = ‘from/because of,’ درد دویدن = ‘the pain of running’ (درد as physical suffering caused by running), and ستوه = an adjective meaning ‘weary/overwrought.’ The sequence gives cause (pain of running) and resultant state (weary).
segments.s285-g57: از این پس is a temporal adverbial phrase meaning “after this / from now on.” مرا زندگانی بود is a classical possessive-existential clause: زندگانی (a noun) + بود (past 'to be') with the experiencer marked by مرا (me) — literally “life was to me,” i.e. “I had life.” Word order places the time phrase up front, then the possessed-noun + copula.
segments.s285-g58: به زنهار رفتن گمانی بود: به زنهار uses به as an adverbial/instrumental marker (“with/as a warning”), زنهار is a noun “warning.” رفتن here has the idiomatic sense “to go” in the euphemistic sense of leaving/loss (i.e. to die), and گمانی بود uses گمانی + بود to mean “there was a suspicion/possibility.” The clause expresses that a feared prospect of departing existed.
segments.s285-g59: خود اندر جهان مرگ را زاده‌ایم: خود is emphatic “ourselves/ours,” اندر جهان is the locative phrase “in this world/this life,” مرگ را marks death as the destined object (را as emphatic/definite marker), and زاده‌ایم is a past perfective meaning “we have been born” (classical passive/experiential sense). Together: “we in the world are born for death.”
segments.s285-g60: بدین کار گردن ترا داده‌ایم: بدین کار (‘for this affair’) is a prepositional phrase (here split in the word list), گردن is used metaphorically as ‘burden/responsibility,’ ترا is the 2nd-person object suffix meaning ‘to you,’ and داده‌ایم is the perfect ‘we have given.’ The whole clause: “we have put the responsibility for this affair on you.”
segments.s285-g61: شنیدستم این داستان از مهان: شنیدستم is an archaic/polished past tense “I heard / I have been told”; the verb comes first as is common in epic diction. این داستان is the direct object, and از مهان marks the source/agents “from the guests/visitors.”
segments.s285-g62: که هرچند باشی به خرم جهان: که introduces a subordinate clause “that,” هرچند is concessive/adversative (“although/however”); باشی is 2nd-person singular subjunctive/present meaning “you may be/remain” (subject is omitted but understood), and به خرم جهان is an adverbial phrase “in the joyful/pleasant world” (به + adjective + noun). The clause expresses a concessive condition about remaining in worldly joy.
segments.s285-g63: سرانجام مرگست زو چاره نیست: سرانجام ‘in the end/finally,’ مرگست is the predicate ‘it is death’ using the archaic -ست copula, ز is the poetic preposition ‘from,’ and چاره نیست is the idiom ‘there is no remedy/answer.’ Taken together: “Ultimately it is death, and there is no remedy for it.”
segments.s285-g64: به من بر بدین جای پیغاره نیست: به من ‘to/for me,’ بر ... جای is the locative/alternant phrase ‘in place of / concerning this place’ (بر جای = in the place of), پیغاره (پیغاره) means ‘message/tidings,’ and نیست negates the clause. The whole means roughly “as for me, there is no message/tidings about taking this place.” Note the poetic splitting of بدین into b + دین in the word list; the sense functions as ‘for/with this.’
segments.s285-g105: ز introduces a source/cause ('for' or 'because of' — poetic use). The phrase 'هفتاد خون گرامی پسر' is a noun phrase: numeral + 'خون' ('blood' used of lineage/bloodshed) + adjective 'گرامی' ('honored') + noun 'پسر' ('sons'). Word order is the typical classical/poetic stacking of modifiers after the numeral.
segments.s285-g106: بنالید is a past tense, third‑person singular of نالیدن ('lament, moan'); the prefixed ب‑ is a poetic/older form. با + NP ('با داور دادگر') marks the addressee/recipient of the lament ('to/with the Judge'), where دادگر postmodifies داور ('the just Judge').
segments.s285-g107: The object 'سرش را' ('his head' + object marker را) precedes the verbal complex. همی here is the archaic/poetic continuous/intentive marker that, with خواست (past 'he wanted'), gives the sense 'he wished/was about to'. 'از تن برید' is the verbal complement ('to cut off from the body'); the bare past form برید follows خواست to express the intended action.
segments.s285-g108: چنان ('so') modifies بدکنش ('ill‑doing / ill‑behaved'); together they form a modifier for the object 'خویشتن را' ('himself' + object marker). The main verb ندید ('did not see') is negative past (ن‑ prefix + دید), so the clause means 'he did not regard/see himself as so ill‑behaved.' This is a poetic inversion where the qualifier precedes the pronoun object.
segments.s285-g109: درفشی ('a standard' / banner) is the direct object that is put in place by the verb کرد ('he did/made/placed'). The phrase 'به بالینش' ('at/near his pillow') gives the location; 'بر پای کرد' is the compound verbal idea (classical split form of برپا کردن) meaning 'to set up/plant' — 'بر' + 'پای کرد' -> 'set up'. Word order (object + locative phrase + verbal nucleus) is typical of poetic diction.
segments.s285-g110: سرش را ('his head' + object marker) is the object of کرد. بدن/بدان here is an archaic contraction functioning like 'به + آن' = 'to/into that', so 'بدان سایه' = 'into/there in that shade'. برجای کرد ('placed in place') means 'laid/left ... in place' — i.e. he laid his head in that shadow. The adjective/locative sequence follows the object before the verb, a frequent poetic ordering.
segments.s285-g111: سوی ('toward') governs the following noun phrase 'لشکر خویش' ('his army' — لشکر + possessive خویش). بنهاد is a past form meaning 'placed/put' or here 'turned/laid' (classical بنهاد روی = 'turned his face'); روی ('face') is the complement showing what was turned toward the army. The sequence is a compact classical phrasing: 'toward his army he set his face.'
segments.s285-g112: چکان is an active/participial form 'dripping' or 'causing to drip' and heads this pictorial clause. 'خون ز بازوش' = 'blood from his arm' (ز = 'from'). The simile 'چون آب جوی' ('like water of a stream') modifies the manner: 'dripping like a stream.' The clause lacks an overt finite verb because the participle provides the visual action, a common poetic device.
segments.s285-g113: همه is a quantifier placed before the noun phrase meaning “all/everyone.” کینه‌جویان is a plural noun (the -ان plural) meaning “revenge-seekers” and is the head of the NP; پرخاشجوی follows and functions as an adjectival/epithet (“quarrelsome/attacking”) modifying that noun. The three words together form a quantified noun phrase: “all the vengeful, quarrelsome ones.”
segments.s285-g114: ز بالا is a poetic prepositional phrase: ز = “from” and بالا = “above/the heights,” so “from above.” به marks direction (“toward/into”) and takes لشکر (“the army/host”) as its object. نهادند is the past tense, 3rd-person plural of نهادن “to place/set”; here it occurs in the idiom روی نهادن (نهادن + روی) meaning “to set one’s face / to turn one’s face (toward).” روی (face) is the thing being set; word order places the verb before روی, but the sense is “they turned their faces toward the army.”
segments.s285-g115: ابا here is the instrument/covering used with the slain (likely the word عبا “cloak/mantle” in poetic spelling); کشتگان is the plural noun “the slain/victims”; بسته is a past participle meaning “bound/tied” and modifies or describes how the slain were secured; بر + پشت + زین is a prepositional phrase “on the back of the saddle” (بر = on/upon, پشت = back, زین = saddle). The whole segment describes how the dead were tied onto the saddles' backs.
segments.s285-g116: بر ایشان = “upon them” (بر + third‑person pronoun ایشان). سرآورده is a verbal/adjectival form meaning “has come about / has befallen” (literally “that which has come to pass”) and here governs the following nouns. پرخاش و کین are the things that have come upon them: پرخاش = reproach/attack (verbal assault) and کین = hatred/vengeance. The clause reads: “Reproach and hatred came upon them.”
segments.s285-g117: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” با کینه‌جویان means “with/against the revenge-seekers” (با marks the opposing party). نبد is an archaic negative copula meaning “was not / there was not” and it precedes the predicate; پهلوان is the predicate noun “champion/hero.” The clause thus means “when there was no champion against the revenge-seekers.”
segments.s285-g118: خروشی is a noun “a cry/roar.” برآمد is the past verb “arose/issued forth.” ز introduces the source (“from”) and پیر و جوان is a fixed pairing meaning “the old and the young” (i.e., everyone). Word order is noun → verb → source: “a cry arose from the old and the young.”
segments.s285-g119: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause (“for/that”). گودرز is the proper name (subject). بر دست پیران is an idiomatic locution: بر + دست + plural noun here idiomatically points to position/standing among that group (“among the elders” or “in the ranks of the elders”). مگر functions as an exception/contrast here (“except/indeed/ nevertheless”) and signals that Goudarz is the notable exception among the elders. The phrase as a whole picks him out as the elder who nonetheless acts.
segments.s285-g120: ز پیری = “because of/from old age” (ز as poetic “from/because of,” پیری = “old age”). به خون اندر آورد سر is a verbal phrase: به خون = “into/onto blood” (به marking direction/into), اندر آورد = “brought into / caused to go into” (اندر + آورد), and سر = “head” (object). Literally “he brought his head into blood,” an idiom here meaning he plunged into/engaged in the bloody fight despite his age. Note the object سر appears at the end, after the verb complex.
segments.s285-g49: زمانه (subject) = 'fortune / the world'; ز تو is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from you' placed before the verb; زود modifies the verb and means 'quickly/soon'; برگاشت is the past/perfective verb meaning 'turned/removed' (here 'turned [its] face away'); روی is the noun 'face' and completes the verbal phrase برگاشتِ روی = 'turned (its) face'. Word order is Subject – PrepPhrase – Adverb – Verb – Object (poetic).
segments.s285-g50: به هنگام is a prepositional phrase 'at the time of'; به here heads that phrase; کینه تو = 'your hatred/grudge' (possessive construction with تو after the noun); چاره مجوی is a negative imperative/prohibitive: مجوی (2nd‑singular negative imperative/subjunctive) 'do not seek' and چاره 'a remedy/solution' is the object. The clause therefore advises: 'At the time of your hatred, do not seek a remedy.'
segments.s285-g51: چو introduces a conditional/time clause ('when' or 'if'); کارت 'your affair/your deed' is the subject; چنین گشت is the verb phrase 'became thus/turned out so' (گشت = became). زنهار خواه is an exhortation: زنهار 'heed / mercy / take heed' used here as a call for clemency and خواه (root 'to want / ask') functions as an imperative/volitive 'ask (for) / beg'. Together: 'If your affair becomes thus, beg for mercy.'
segments.s285-g52: بدان is the compound imperative/vocative built from the prefix ب- plus دان 'know' (i.e. 'know this'); تات is the person/people referred to; زنده is an adjective 'alive'; برم is 1st‑singular present/future of برد in the sense 'I will take/bring'; نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of the king'. Full sense: 'Know that I will bring that Tat alive before the king.'
segments.s285-g53: ببخشاید is a 3rd‑person present/subjunctive/optative form meaning 'may he/they forgive' or simply 'he/they forgive'; از دل is an adverbial 'from the heart' (sincere forgiveness); همی is an archaic/epic particle marking continued or emphatic action; بر تو is the prepositional object 'toward you' (the sequence ends with an additional بر for rhythmic/poetic balance). The clause thus says that forgiveness will be given sincerely to you.
segments.s285-g54: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for / because'; هستی here is the 2nd‑person present of 'to be' ('you are'); جهان‌پهلوان is the predicate noun 'world‑champion' (the title); سر به سر is an idiom 'head to head' → 'through and through / completely' reinforcing the excellence. Grammatically: 'because you are the world‑champion through and through.'
segments.s285-g55: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (to that person); گفت here is the verb 'said' (the provided sense list lacked the verbal sense, so use -1); پیران is the subject 'the elders'; که introduces the content clause; این خود مباد is an optative/negative wish: این 'this', خود 'itself' for emphasis, مباد 'may it not be'. Together: 'The elders said to him, "May this not be so."'
segments.s285-g56: به فرجام 'at/in the end' (به + فرجام); بر من is the prepositional object 'upon me / befall me'; چنین بد 'such a bad thing' (چنین 'thus/such', بد 'bad/evil'); مباد is the negative optative 'may it not be'. The clause is a further wish: 'May such ill not befall me in the end.'

sentences.s285-s93: They departed with the slain just the same, and drove the armored troop to flee on foot.
sentences.s285-s21: You act like hunted game before me — where is that host, the chiefs of the assembly?
sentences.s285-s22: No friend came to you from the host, and among them I see no rescuer.
sentences.s285-s23: Where, then, are all that strength and manliness, the arms and heart and treasure and wisdom?
sentences.s285-s24: Those pillars that were Afrasiab’s support — now the sun has grown dark for the king.
sentences.s285-s61: The whole host wailed in lamentation, because the throng had not seen the hero.
sentences.s285-s62: A banner arose from a dark cloud, — a swarm of boars and raiders rushed into the plain of battle.
sentences.s285-s63: From the camp the drum's voice rose; its sound kept sending kisses upon the sky.
sentences.s285-s64: The great ones approached the hero; they came full of laughter and rejoicing.
sentences.s285-s81: His army, freed from toil and weariness, remained; my army likewise remained thus, in haste.
sentences.s285-s82: But I have such a hope that the world's sovereign, my sun, will come.
sentences.s285-s83: He will illumine this battlefield with (his) glory, and will bring an army newly bent on vengeance.
sentences.s285-s84: I have sent a certain wise man; I have given the king many counsels.
sentences.s285-s37: He hurled the dagger like an arrow; it struck the old commander's arm.
sentences.s285-s38: When Goudarz grew tired at his hand, out of grudge he turned his face into anger.
sentences.s285-s39: He flung his gambeson at the elders; the armor on his body was torn to pieces.
sentences.s285-s40: From behind he came toward the heart; he roared and, distraught, turned his head back.
sentences.s285-s41: Blood from his liver rose into his mouth; his spirit, too, left him at the same moment.
sentences.s285-s42: When the living lion came to his head, he cried out to the just judge.
sentences.s285-s43: On that flinty mountain his heart/pulse beat for a while; after the vengeance, the battlefield rested.
sentences.s285-s44: Fortune (time) has lent poison to the water; its claw rends the lion's heart and the leopard's hide.
sentences.s285-s45: Such is the very turn of fate; it does not heed the teacher's counsel.
sentences.s285-s46: When Goudarz climbed up onto that mountainside,
sentences.s285-s47: his heart and hand torn, and his head prostrate on the earth, his weapon broken and his waist unbent/broken,
sentences.s285-s48: Thus Goudarz spoke: 'O manly lion, head of the champions and pillar of the brave,'
sentences.s285-s17: He ran up onto that mountain-top, from which perhaps the champion might return.
sentences.s285-s18: Goudarz looked and wept bitterly; he feared the vicissitudes of fate.
sentences.s285-s19: He realized that there is no fidelity between people; relations are kept closed for the sake of injustice.
sentences.s285-s20: He cried aloud, 'O famed champion, what was it that made you run on foot up to here?'
sentences.s285-s33: Goudarz kept circling the mountain; there was no path for him, and he grew weary.
sentences.s285-s34: He was on foot and took up his shield, like the herdsmen who seized (theirs).
sentences.s285-s35: With his shield grasped before him and his gambeson in hand, he had raised his head up from the low place.
sentences.s285-s36: The elders saw him from afar; one (or someone) sprang up from beside the rock of the commander Tur.
sentences.s285-s65: Thus the host spoke; yet the champion turned away from him, his spirit darkened.
sentences.s285-s66: For among the elders one was a lion‑hearted man; year after year he sought to bring (him).
sentences.s285-s67: Thus the hero recalled at that time, and entrusted to him the ear (the attention) of both old and young.
sentences.s285-s68: With his finger he pointed to the place of battle and said, 'See what the times have done to him.'
sentences.s285-s69: He ordered Roham to mount; bringing him in, he closed the gap between them.
sentences.s285-s70: He said to him, 'Bind him to the saddle; bring him like the Arab coursers on Nāvand.'
sentences.s285-s71: Leave his banner and arms just as they are; do not lay any hand on his armor or his chest.
sentences.s285-s72: In that way, remembering how the champion had acted, Roham rushed forth like a gale.
sentences.s285-s85: If the king brings the Turks with an army, we will have no foothold in this place of hatred.
